Osun State Government · Q1–Q3 2025

Where Does
The Money
Go?

A data-driven account of the 2025 budget: what was promised, what was collected, and what was built. All figures sourced directly from official government records. No estimates. No projections.

Author
Adediran Adeyemi
Website
www.adediranadeyemi.com
Data Period
January – September 2025
Source
Official Osun State Budget Records

A note on this report

What Citizens Deserve to Know

Every year, the Osun State Government prepares a budget. It is a document full of promises: classrooms to be built, drugs to be procured, farmers to be supported, women to be protected. And every year, most citizens have no idea how much of it actually happened.

This report draws from the official government quarterly performance data for 2025. The numbers here are not ours. They belong to the government. We have simply organised them so that a schoolteacher in Ede, a trader in Osogbo, and a lawmaker in Abuja can all read the same page and reach their own conclusions.

This is not opposition propaganda. Every figure cited traces back to the official Osun State Budget Performance Report for Q1–Q3 2025. If the numbers are uncomfortable, the discomfort belongs to the performance, not to the reporting.

The report covers nine major areas: where revenue comes from, how the budget is spent, what sectors are receiving attention and which are being ignored, the true cost of debt, and a complete scorecard of every major budget line. Read it. Share it. Ask questions.

Section 01

State Finances at a Glance

₦427.7B
Total 2025 Budget
₦202.6B
Total Spent (YTD)
47.4%
Budget Execution Rate
₦250.4B
Revenue Collected

As of the end of September 2025, Osun State had budgeted ₦427.7 billion for the full year. Of that, ₦202.6 billion had been spent, a rate of 47.4 percent. At first glance, that figure aligns reasonably with three-quarters of the fiscal year. But the aggregate number becomes more revealing when broken down by expenditure type.

Recurrent obligations—salaries and debt servicing—have largely been met. Capital expenditure, however, which covers infrastructure, healthcare facilities, schools, and agricultural investments, stands at 34.9 percent of its budgeted allocation. This divergence between recurrent and capital performance points to a pattern worth examining.

Put differently, the government has executed nearly half of its overall budget, but only about a third of its capital commitments.

Section 02

Where Does the Money Come From?

Before asking where money goes, a citizen should ask where it comes from. The answer, in Osun's case, is mostly from Abuja.

Figure 1: Revenue sources, budgeted vs. collected, Q1–Q3 2025.

The Federal Allocation Problem

Of the ₦397.7 billion total revenue budget, ₦251.7 billion, which is 63 percent, was expected to arrive from the Federal Government's FAAC distribution. This is money shared from national oil revenues, VAT, and other federal pools. Osun has collected ₦198 billion of that, a rate of 78.7 percent, which is acceptable.

The real problem is what the state generates for itself. Independent revenue was budgeted at ₦109.9 billion. Only ₦45.9 billion has been collected. That is 41.7 percent. The state is generating less than half of what it planned from its own economy.

Figure 2: Osun's independent revenue breakdown, budget vs. actual.

The Informal Sector: Untapped and Abandoned

The informal sector tax had a budget of ₦5.9 billion. Only ₦402 million was collected. That is 6.8 percent. Millions of traders, artisans, and small business owners in Osun are almost completely outside the tax net, and no serious effort has been made to reach them.

Performance in aid and grant inflows is notably below expectations. Out of ₦30.7 billion projected from international and development partners, ₦6.3 billion has been received, representing 20.6 percent of the target. In addition, no collections have been recorded from the ₦3.5 billion budgeted for tax arrears during the period under review.

A state that cannot fund itself from within will always be at the mercy of decisions made in Abuja. When oil prices fall and FAAC allocations are cut, Osun has no buffer. That is not a theoretical risk. It has happened before.

Section 03

How Is the Money Being Spent?

Figure 3: Budget composition and execution rate by expenditure category.
₦102.9B
Personnel Budget
64.5%
Personnel Execution
₦191.5B
Capital Budget
34.9%
Capital Execution

The ₦427.7 billion budget splits into four main buckets. Personnel costs account for ₦102.9 billion. Overhead takes ₦88.9 billion. Other recurrent spending adds ₦44.4 billion. Capital expenditure is the largest single item at ₦191.5 billion.

The execution rates reveal the priority order. Personnel at 64.5 percent: government workers are being paid. Overhead at 49.9 percent: offices are running. Capital at 34.9 percent: construction and investment are lagging badly. Three quarters through the year, only a third of capital projects are done.

The pattern is consistent across Nigerian state governments: the parts of the budget that benefit public servants are executed well. The parts that benefit the public are not.

Section 04

Who Is Building What?

Capital spending is where government either earns or loses its credibility. It is the investment that determines whether hospitals get built, whether roads become passable, and whether children sit on chairs in proper classrooms.

Figure 4: Capital budget vs. actual spending by sector.

Road Infrastructure: Allocation Size and Implementation Status

The single largest capital allocation covers road infrastructure at ₦117.9 billion. ₦50.6 billion, or 42.9 percent, has been spent. At three quarters of the year, it remains below the midpoint.

Education: Good on Paper, Slow in Practice

Education received ₦27.3 billion in capital allocation. Only ₦7.8 billion, or 28.6 percent, has been spent. The school infrastructure sub-programme has a ₦22.3 billion budget and has spent ₦7.1 billion, or 31.7 percent. Hundreds of school buildings remain unfinished or unstarted.

Agriculture: The Biggest Disappointment

Agriculture received ₦5.9 billion for capital investment. Only ₦528 million has been spent. That is 9 percent. In a state where a large portion of the population depends on farming for income, this is not a budget shortfall. It is a choice.

Fisheries development had a budget of ₦333 million. Zero naira was spent. Post-harvest loss reduction had ₦272 million budgeted. Zero naira was spent. The government budgeted for a food-secure Osun and then did nothing to build it.

Section 05

Debt Service and Outstanding Obligations

Figure 5: Debt repayment vs. social and development spending, actual YTD.
₦29.0B
Total Debt Budget
61.2%
Debt Execution Rate
₦17.8B
Debt Paid YTD
₦6.6B
Health Capital Budget

The Debt Management Office is one of the most efficient units in the Osun State Government. Of the ₦29 billion set aside for debt repayment, ₦17.8 billion, or 61.2 percent, has already been paid. This money is going to banks and bond markets, not to citizens.

Compare that to the entire capital budget for health at ₦6.6 billion. Debt repayment in nine months alone is 2.7 times what Osun has budgeted to build hospitals and expand health services for the entire year. The state pays its lenders faster than it builds clinics.

Women and children's programmes received ₦50 million and spent ₦1.99 million of it, a rate of 4 percent. Gender equity overhead was ₦2.34 billion and only 3.6 percent was spent.

Section 06

Health: Staff on Duty, Drugs Not in Stock

Health spending in Osun is a story of two parallel realities. The sector received a total budget of ₦31.2 billion across all categories. The execution rate is 54.6 percent, which is near the midpoint. But that average conceals an urgent imbalance.

Personnel costs for health were budgeted at ₦16 billion and have been paid at a rate of 67.5 percent. Health workers are receiving salaries. Emergency preparedness is at 92.6 percent execution, well ahead of schedule.

Then there is the medicine and vaccine procurement line. Budgeted at ₦2.24 billion, it has been executed at only 35.2 percent. The health workers are in the facility. The drugs may not be.

A government hospital without medicine is a building with staff. ₦1.45 billion in drug procurement remains unspent three quarters of the way through the year. That is a procurement failure.

Health infrastructure is at 52.8 percent. Construction is happening, though it is behind the nine-month mark. Evidence generation and health data systems are at 16.9 percent, meaning the government is investing very little in knowing what is actually happening in its own health system.

Section 07

Education: Teachers Paid, Classrooms Unfinished

Figure 6: Education budget execution by programme, Q1–Q3 2025.

Education is the most funded sector after road infrastructure. Total education expenditure reaches ₦57.8 billion. The execution rate of 53.4 percent is near the average for this period. But the breakdown tells a very different story depending on which part of education you are in.

Teacher salaries are the standout success: ₦19.4 billion budgeted, 85.9 percent paid. If you are a teacher in Osun, you are most likely receiving your salary.

School infrastructure, however, is a different picture. ₦22.3 billion was allocated for building and upgrading school facilities. Only 31.7 percent has been spent. ₦15.3 billion worth of school building remains on paper.

ICT and Digital Learning: Zero Progress

The government budgeted ₦52 million to establish ICT learning centres in selected schools. As of September 2025, not a single naira of that has been spent. A full budget line for digital education, sitting unused.

The equity and inclusion in education line had ₦17.9 million budgeted. Zero naira was spent. The government decided, through inaction, that educational equity was not a priority this year.

Figure 7: Agriculture sector execution, showing the pay-vs-do gap.

Section 08

Security: Consistently Over Budget

Figure 8: Security-related budget lines vs. actual spending.

Security spending stands apart from almost every other line in this budget. Across personnel costs, overhead, and other recurrent expenditure, security-related programmes are consistently over or near the top of their budget limits while most other sectors lag far behind.

The Public Order and Safety personnel line was budgeted at ₦195 million. It has been spent at 366 percent of budget. That is ₦715 million against a ₦195 million plan. Disaster preparedness personnel are at 147 percent.

Security overspending is not automatically wrong. But when security personnel costs run at 366 percent of budget while women and children's programmes run at 4 percent, the citizen is entitled to ask whether the numbers are planned or improvised.

The absence of a supplementary budget or public explanation for security overruns means there is no transparent accounting for where this extra spending went. The data shows the money left. It does not show what it bought.

Section 09

The Complete Scorecard

The chart below consolidates every major finding in this report into a single reference. Green means the budget is being executed at a healthy rate. Gold means it is lagging. Red means it is either failing or wildly over budget.

Figure 9: Complete budget performance scorecard, Q1–Q3 2025.

Conclusion

Five Things the Numbers Tell Us

01
Osun depends on Abuja for survival.
63 percent of revenue comes from federal allocations. The state's own tax collection is failing. A state that cannot fund itself cannot plan for itself.
02
Capital projects are the budget's weakest link.
At 34.9 percent execution, the investment side of government is running one third of the way through the year's work. That is the gap between what citizens were promised and what they can see on the ground.
03
Debt is being paid faster than hospitals are being built.
₦17.8 billion has gone to debt service while the entire capital health budget is ₦6.6 billion. Whether the original loans were borrowed for the right things is a question citizens can and should ask.
04
Agriculture is largely abandoned.
With 9 percent capital execution and 2 percent on actual farm activities, the sector that feeds Osun's people is receiving staff salaries and very little else.
05
Social protection for the most vulnerable is nearly invisible.
Women and children's programmes, gender equity, and social welfare have some of the lowest execution rates in the entire budget. Their near-zero spending is a political choice.

A government that publishes this data is a government that, at minimum, is not hiding these numbers. That is worth acknowledging. What citizens do with these numbers is the next part of the story.

Methodology

Data Notes & Sources

Source
All figures are drawn from the official Osun State Government Budget Performance Report, Q1–Q3 2025 (January 1 to September 30, 2025), and the Osun State data portal: https://openstates.ng/osun/data?lgas=true.
Files used
19 official Excel workbooks covering revenue by administrative and economic classification, expenditure by administrative, functional, economic and programme classification, personnel, overhead, capital, and other recurrent spending.
Rounding
All naira figures are rounded to one decimal place in billions (B) or millions (M) for readability. Full precision figures are available in the original source files.
% Execution
All percentages represent actual spending YTD (Q1–Q3) as a share of the full-year original budget. Figures above 100 percent indicate spending that exceeded the annual allocation.
Limitations
This report does not assess procurement integrity or physical project completion. Budget execution is a financial measure. It does not confirm that funds were spent effectively or honestly.
Tools
Produced using Python: pandas, matplotlib, reportlab. Charts embedded as base64 images. No external data calls are made by this file.
